section.title: Comfort for the Contrite
section.sectionRationale: Right after promising a house of prayer for all nations, Isaiah turns to indict Israel's own failed shepherds and idolatrous people, blind watchmen, greedy dogs, child sacrifice in the valleys, before landing on one of the most tender verses in the whole book, the High and Lofty One who dwells with the contrite and lowly in spirit. It is judgment and comfort in the same breath.
section.thesis: God exposes Israel's failed shepherds and shameless idolatry without flinching, yet the same high and holy God who inhabits eternity chooses to dwell with the crushed and lowly in spirit, promising peace to those far and near while insisting there is no peace for the wicked.
section.frameNote: Four movements: blind watchmen and greedy shepherds; the righteous perishing unnoticed; Israel's shameless idolatry exposed; and the High and Lofty One who dwells with the contrite, offering peace, peace to those far and near.

verses.text: Come, all you beasts of the field; eat greedily, all you beasts of the forest.
Israel’s watchmen are blind, they are all oblivious; they are all mute dogs, they cannot bark; they are dreamers lying around, loving to slumber.
Like ravenous dogs, they are never satisfied. They are shepherds with no discernment; they all turn to their own way, each one seeking his own gain:
“Come, let me get the wine, let us imbibe the strong drink, and tomorrow will be like today, only far better!”

units.label: The righteous perish, no one takes it to heart
units.note: guided from the presence of evil

verses.text: The righteous perish, and no one takes it to heart; devout men are swept away, while no one considers that the righteous are guided from the presence of evil.
Those who walk uprightly enter into peace; they find rest, lying down in death.

units.label: Sons of a sorceress
units.note: idolatry, child sacrifice, exhausting pursuit of false gods

verses.text: “But come here, you sons of a sorceress, you offspring of adulterers and prostitutes!
Whom are you mocking? At whom do you snarl and stick out your tongue? Are you not children of transgression, offspring of deceit,
who burn with lust among the oaks, under every luxuriant tree, who slaughter your children in the valleys, under the clefts of the rocks?
Your portion is among the smooth stones of the valley; indeed, they are your lot. Even to them you have poured out a drink offering and offered a grain offering. Should I relent because of these?
On a high and lofty hill you have made your bed, and there you went up to offer sacrifices.

Behind the door and doorpost you have set up your memorial. Forsaking Me, you uncovered your bed; you climbed up and opened it wide. And you have made a pact with those whose bed you have loved; you have gazed upon their nakedness.
You went to Molech with oil and multiplied your perfumes. You have sent your envoys a great distance; you have descended even to Sheol itself.
You are wearied by your many journeys, but you did not say, “There is no hope!” You found renewal of your strength; therefore you did not grow weak.
Whom have you dreaded and feared, so that you lied and failed to remember Me or take this to heart? Is it not because I have long been silent that you do not fear Me?
I will expose your righteousness and your works, and they will not profit you.

When you cry out, let your companies of idols deliver you! Yet the wind will carry off all of them, a breath will take them away. But he who seeks refuge in Me will inherit the land and possess My holy mountain.”

units.label: I dwell with the contrite in spirit
units.note: peace, peace to those far and near

verses.text: And it will be said, “Build it up, build it up, prepare the way, take every obstacle out of the way of My people.”

For thus says the One who is high and lifted up, who inhabits eternity, whose name is Holy: “I dwell in a high and holy place, and with the oppressed and {{shafal|humble}} in spirit, to restore the spirit of the lowly and revive the heart of the {{nidkeh|contrite}}.
For I will not accuse you forever, nor will I always be angry; for then the spirit of man would grow weak before Me, with the breath of those I have made.
I was enraged by his sinful greed, so I struck him and hid My face in anger; yet he kept turning back to the desires of his heart.
I have seen his ways, but I will heal him; I will guide him and restore comfort to him and his mourners,
bringing praise to their lips. Peace, peace to those far and near,” says the LORD, “and I will heal them.”
But the wicked are like the storm-tossed sea, for it cannot be still, and its waves churn up mire and muck.
“There is no peace,” says my God, “for the wicked.”

shafal.gloss: Humble, low, brought down. God, who is Himself high and lifted up, chooses deliberately to dwell with the one who is shaphal, low in spirit, an intentional condescension of the exalted God toward the humbled soul, the same movement Paul later describes of Christ, Philippians 2:8.

nidkeh.gloss: Crushed, contrite, struck down in spirit. God revives the heart of the nakeh, the one crushed by grief or guilt; the same root and idea appears in Psalm 34:18, the LORD is near to the brokenhearted, and shapes Jesus' own beatitude, blessed are the poor in spirit, Matthew 5:3.

basic.context: Isaiah moves from Israel's failed leaders, watchmen too blind to warn and shepherds too greedy to care, to shocking idolatry, child sacrifice and shrine prostitution among the very people who claimed God's name. Yet the chapter turns, without losing its honesty about sin, to one of the tenderest promises in the book: the high and holy God dwells with the crushed and lowly.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage refuses to soften either truth: sin is named plainly and without excuse, watchmen who cannot bark, sacrifice to Molech, yet God's nearness to the humble is offered just as plainly, without conditions of achievement, only brokenness and turning to Him. It closes with a stark, unremovable line, there is no peace for the wicked, that must be held alongside the comfort.
basic.originalLanguages: God is described dwelling with the shafal, the humble and brought-low, and reviving the heart of the nidkeh, the crushed or contrite. These are not abstractions; they describe real people, brought low by real guilt or grief, whom the eternal God stoops to meet.
basic.typology: The high and lofty One who dwells with the lowly anticipates the incarnation itself, God taking on lowliness to dwell among the humble, and the peace, peace to those far and near anticipates the peace Christ makes between Jew and Gentile at the cross.
basic.christ: Paul quotes this exact phrase, peace to those who were far and peace to those who were near, in Ephesians 2:17, applying it directly to the peace Christ achieves between Jew and Gentile through His blood. Jesus' own beatitude, blessed are the poor in spirit, and blessed are those who mourn, Matthew 5:3-4, echoes the promise that God restores the lowly and comforts mourners.
basic.god: God does not deny His own anger at persistent, self-destructive sin, I was enraged, I struck him, yet He insists He will not accuse forever, choosing instead to heal, guide, and comfort those who turn back to Him, a holiness that judges and a nearness that restores held together without contradiction.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 2:17 quotes this passage of the peace Christ brings to those far and near. Psalm 34:18 echoes God's nearness to the brokenhearted and crushed in spirit. Matthew 5:3-4 pictures the same comfort for the poor in spirit and those who mourn.
basic.oneStory: God exposes His people's worst failures without flinching, then still stoops from His high and holy dwelling to revive the crushed and comfort the mourner. That same movement, holiness descending to dwell with the humble, reaches its fullness when God becomes flesh in Christ and makes peace for those far and near at the cross.

context.literary: Moves from prophetic satire against corrupt leaders, dogs and drunken shepherds, through a lament that the righteous perish unnoticed, into a scathing indictment of idolatry using marital unfaithfulness as its central metaphor, before resolving in one of Isaiah's most tender oracles of divine nearness to the humble.
context.historical: Addressed to a community whose leadership had failed morally and spiritually, and which had absorbed pagan fertility and Molech worship practices, including child sacrifice, condemned repeatedly elsewhere in the prophets, 2 Kings 23:10, Jeremiah 32:35.
context.canonical: Its closing promise, peace, peace to those far and near, is picked up directly by Paul in Ephesians 2 to describe the reconciling work of Christ, one of the clearest Old Testament anticipations of the gospel's reach to both Jew and Gentile.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the corruption of Israel's leaders and the depth of the nation's idolatry honestly, without excuse, while still holding open a door of comfort and healing to anyone who turns from that sin to seek refuge in the LORD.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The oracle both describes God's righteous anger at sin and prescribes the response it calls for, seek refuge in Me, verse 13, turn from your own way, implicit throughout, promising restoration to those who do.

debates.question: How can God be described as high and lifted up, inhabiting eternity, and also dwelling with the lowly, in the same verse?

views.label: Paradox resolved in condescension
views.case: God's transcendence and His nearness are not in tension; His greatness is precisely displayed in His willingness to stoop to the humble, a pattern culminating in the incarnation, where the eternal Word takes on lowliness, John 1:14, Philippians 2:6-8.

views.label: Two distinct dwellings, not literal contradiction
views.case: The verse pictures God as having two dwelling places, His high and holy heaven, and a chosen nearness to crushed hearts, meaning both are true simultaneously without either compromising the other.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms the same central truth: God's transcendent holiness does not distance Him from the humble, it is the very reason His nearness to them is so remarkable and comforting.

study.originalLanguages: God dwells with the shafal, the humbled and brought low, and He revives the heart of the nidkeh, the crushed or contrite. Neither word describes achievement; both describe a posture of brokenness that God Himself chooses to meet and restore, not from a distance but by dwelling near.

typology.type: The High and Lofty One who dwells with the lowly, 57:15
typology.fulfillment: The incarnation, God the Son taking on humility to dwell among humbled humanity, Philippians 2:6-8, John 1:14.

typology.type: Peace, peace to those far and near, 57:19
typology.fulfillment: Christ's reconciling work between Jew and Gentile at the cross, Ephesians 2:14-17.

study.christ: Paul quotes 57:19 directly in Ephesians 2:17, He came and preached peace to you who were far away and peace to those who were near, naming Christ as the one who makes this ancient promise concrete, breaking down the dividing wall of hostility between Jew and Gentile through His own blood. And the pattern of 57:15, the High and Holy One stooping to dwell with the lowly, is precisely what happens in the incarnation, when the eternal Word takes on flesh to dwell among the very people He comes to restore, Philippians 2:6-8.
study.god: God does not excuse sin, He names Israel's idolatry and her leaders' failures in unsparing detail, yet His anger is not the final word; He promises to heal, guide, and comfort those who turn to Him, while still insisting, without contradiction, that there is no peace for those who refuse to turn, verse 21.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes 57:19 directly of Christ's peace between Jew and Gentile.
Christ's condescension mirrors the High and Lofty One who dwells with the lowly.
The LORD is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit.
Jesus' beatitudes echo God's promised comfort for the lowly and mourning.

study.oneStory: Israel's leaders failed and her people chased idols into the valleys, yet the same God who exposes all of it still says He dwells with the crushed and lowly, promising peace to those far and near. That promise becomes flesh in Christ, who takes on lowliness Himself and, at the cross, makes the peace this passage foretold between everyone once far from God and near.

sermon.title: Grace Amid Frailty: God's Presence with the Broken
sermon.why: Carson works through Isaiah 56-57 in three portraits, a righteous God who welcomes contrite sinners, an idolatrous people, and the promise of God's presence with the broken, centering on 57:15's picture of the transcendent God who dwells with the contrite and lowly in spirit, the heart of this passage.

quiz.q: What failure does Isaiah name in Israel's watchmen and shepherds, 56:9-12?

options.text: They are blind, silent, self-seeking, and given to drunken pleasure instead of warning and guarding the people.
options.note: Verses 10-12 describe watchmen who cannot bark and shepherds seeking only their own gain.

options.text: They are too strict in enforcing God's law.
options.note: The failure is neglect and self-indulgence, not excessive strictness.

options.text: They are too young and inexperienced to lead.
options.note: The text describes moral failure, not inexperience.

options.text: They are persecuted for warning the people too much.
options.note: This is the opposite of what the text says; they fail to warn at all, described as mute dogs who cannot bark.

quiz.q: What does God mean by saying He dwells 'with the oppressed and humble in spirit,' 57:15?

options.text: The high and holy God chooses to draw near and revive those who are crushed and lowly in spirit.
options.note: The verse pairs God's transcendence with His deliberate nearness to the humble.

options.text: God only associates with the powerful and self-sufficient.
options.note: The verse says the opposite; He dwells with the lowly and contrite.

options.text: God is physically confined to a single earthly location.
options.note: The verse emphasizes His transcendence, inhabiting eternity, alongside His chosen nearness.

options.text: It refers only to God's presence in the Jerusalem temple.
options.note: The temple is one place God's presence dwelt, but this verse speaks of God's posture toward the humble in spirit generally, not one building.

quiz.q: How does Paul use Isaiah 57:19, 'peace, peace to those far and near,' in Ephesians 2?

options.text: He applies it to Christ's work of reconciling Jew and Gentile into one people through His blood.
options.note: Ephesians 2:14-17 quotes this phrase directly of Christ's peace-making work.

options.text: He applies it only to physical distance between cities.
options.note: Paul applies it to spiritual reconciliation, not literal geography.

options.text: He uses it to argue that Gentiles remain permanently excluded from God's people.
options.note: This is the opposite of Paul's whole argument, which is full inclusion of Gentiles through Christ's peace.

quiz.q: How does the passage close in 57:20-21, and why does it matter?

options.text: It insists there is no peace for the wicked, holding judgment and comfort together without contradiction.
options.note: The promise of comfort to the contrite does not erase the reality of judgment for the unrepentant.

options.text: It promises peace to everyone regardless of repentance.
options.note: The text explicitly denies peace to the wicked who do not turn.

options.text: It contradicts the earlier promise of healing in verse 18-19.
options.note: It can read as tension, but the two truths sit together consistently: healing for those who turn, no peace for those who refuse.

options.text: It refers only to a temporary lack of political peace, not spiritual standing.
options.note: The context is about spiritual restoration and refusal, not merely politics.

questions: Israel's watchmen were blind and silent when they should have warned. Where might you be staying silent when you should be speaking up in love?

The chapter names Israel's idolatry in raw, uncomfortable detail. What is the modern equivalent chase, the exhausting pursuit of something that leaves you wearied but unwilling to admit there is no hope in it?
God says He dwells with the contrite and lowly in spirit. Are you trying to appear strong before God rather than coming to Him crushed and honest?
Peace, peace to those far and near is fulfilled in Christ reconciling Jew and Gentile. Who feels far from you, or far from God, that you could help draw near through the peace of Christ?
The chapter ends starkly, there is no peace for the wicked. Is there an area of ongoing, unrepented sin in your life quietly robbing you of the peace God offers to the contrite?

translationNotes.note: Molech was the Ammonite deity associated with child sacrifice, condemned repeatedly in the Torah, Leviticus 18:21, 20:2-5; the reference here confirms Israel's absorption of this practice despite the law's clear prohibition.
High and lofty renders the same Hebrew phrase used of the LORD Himself in 6:1, deliberately echoing Isaiah's own inaugural vision of God's transcendence, now paired with His nearness to the lowly.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 56:9-57:21; no em dashes present in this passage. Sermon verified this session at thegospelcoalition.org.
